4.1.1

Globalisation

Globalisation — the growing economic interdependence between countries — has reshaped how goods, services, capital, and labour move across borders, driven by a specific combination of technological, political, and economic forces.

Globalisation
The increasing economic integration and interdependence of national economies, through growing flows of trade, capital (investment), labour (migration), and information across borders.
Multinational Corporation (MNC)
A firm that owns and controls productive assets (factories, subsidiaries) in more than one country, as distinct from a firm that merely exports to foreign markets.
Driving Force of GlobalisationExplanation
Trade liberalisationProgressive reductions in tariffs and quotas, driven by the WTO and regional trading blocs (Section 5), have made cross-border trade dramatically cheaper and easier.
Falling transport costsContainerisation and larger, more efficient cargo ships have sharply reduced the cost of shipping goods internationally.
Technological changeThe internet and digital communication allow instantaneous coordination of complex global supply chains, and enable trade in services that once had to be delivered in person.
Growth of MNCsMultinationals have driven foreign direct investment and cross-border production networks, actively organising global supply chains rather than simply responding to them.
Financial market liberalisationReduced capital controls have allowed investment capital to flow far more freely between countries.

Benefits of Globalisation

  • Access to larger markets allows firms to exploit economies of scale, lowering costs and prices for consumers worldwide.
  • Greater specialisation according to comparative advantage (Section 2) raises global productive efficiency.
  • Foreign direct investment can bring capital, technology, and jobs to developing economies (Section 17).
  • Increased competition can drive innovation and product choice.

Costs of Globalisation

  • Structural unemployment in industries unable to compete with lower-cost foreign producers (companion BIZ-OMICS Unemployment guide).
  • Increased inequality both within and between countries, as globalisation's gains are not evenly distributed (Section 14).
  • Loss of domestic policy autonomy, as governments compete to attract mobile MNC investment (e.g. via lower corporation tax, Section 22).
  • Environmental costs from increased global production and transport.
  • Tax avoidance by MNCs using transfer pricing to shift profits to low-tax jurisdictions, eroding the domestic tax base.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: MNC Transfer Pricing
Multinational corporations can artificially set the prices charged between their own subsidiaries in different countries (transfer pricing) to shift reported profit towards low-tax jurisdictions and away from higher-tax countries where genuine economic activity occurs — a major and continuing source of tension between MNCs and national governments seeking to protect their tax base (Section 22), and a key driver of international efforts towards a global minimum corporate tax rate.
Deglobalisation and "Slowbalisation": globalisation's advance has not been continuous or irreversible — the 2008 financial crisis, rising geopolitical tensions, the COVID-19 pandemic's exposure of fragile global supply chains, and a resurgence of protectionist sentiment (Section 6) in several major economies have all contributed to a marked slowdown, and in some measures a partial reversal, of global economic integration since around 2010 — a trend some economists term "slowbalisation" rather than full deglobalisation, since global trade and investment flows remain historically high even if their growth has stalled.
4.1.2

Specialisation and Trade

The economic case for international trade rests on the theory of comparative advantage — the insight that countries can gain from trade even when one country is more efficient than another at producing everything.

Absolute Advantage
A country has absolute advantage in producing a good if it can produce more of that good than another country, using the same quantity of resources.
Comparative Advantage
A country has comparative advantage in producing a good if it can produce that good at a lower opportunity cost (in terms of the other good forgone) than another country — even if it does not hold absolute advantage in anything.

Worked Example — Comparative Advantage

Using fixed resources, Country A can produce either 100 units of cloth or 50 units of wine. Country B can produce either 60 units of cloth or 40 units of wine.

Country A's opportunity cost of 1 cloth = 50÷100 = 0.5 wine
Country B's opportunity cost of 1 cloth = 40÷60 = 0.67 wine
→ Country A has comparative advantage in cloth (lower opportunity cost)

Country A's opportunity cost of 1 wine = 100÷50 = 2 cloth
Country B's opportunity cost of 1 wine = 60÷40 = 1.5 cloth
→ Country B has comparative advantage in wine (lower opportunity cost)

Key point: Country A holds absolute advantage in both goods (it can produce more of each with the same resources) — yet both countries can still gain by specialising according to comparative advantage (A in cloth, B in wine) and trading, since this allows total combined output of both goods to exceed what either could achieve alone at their pre-trade production mix.

Assumption of the Comparative Advantage ModelReal-World Limitation
No transport costsReal shipping and logistics costs can offset some or all of the theoretical gains from trade.
Perfect factor mobility within a countryWorkers cannot always easily retrain or relocate to the newly-favoured export industry (structural unemployment, companion Unemployment guide).
Constant opportunity costsIn reality, opportunity costs typically rise as an economy specialises further, limiting the extent of beneficial specialisation.
Free trade with no barriersTariffs, quotas, and other protectionist measures (Sections 6–7) can prevent the theoretical gains from being realised.
Why Comparative Advantage Remains the Core Case for Free Trade: despite its simplifying assumptions, comparative advantage remains the fundamental economic argument for international trade and against protectionism (Sections 6–7) — it explains why trade can benefit both parties even between a highly developed and a much less developed economy, provided each specialises according to its own relative (not absolute) efficiency.
4.1.3

Pattern of Trade

The pattern of trade — which countries trade which goods with which partners — has shifted substantially over recent decades, reflecting changing comparative advantage, globalisation (Section 1), and the rise of new economic powers.

Trend in the Global Pattern of TradeExplanation
Rising share of trade involving emerging economiesChina, India, and other emerging economies have captured a rapidly growing share of world trade, particularly in manufactured goods, as their productive capacity and infrastructure have developed (Section 17).
Growth of trade in intermediate goods and global supply chainsRather than a finished product being made entirely in one country, components increasingly cross multiple borders during production — a single smartphone may include parts sourced from a dozen or more countries before final assembly.
Growing share of trade in servicesDigital technology (Section 1) has made previously "untradeable" services — software, consulting, financial services, media — increasingly tradeable across borders.
Regional trade concentrationA large share of world trade occurs between geographically proximate countries, often within trading blocs (Section 5), reflecting lower transport costs and harmonised regulation.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: The Rise of China in Global Trade
China's share of global manufacturing exports rose dramatically from the 1980s onward, following market-oriented economic reforms beginning in 1978 and its 2001 accession to the WTO (Section 5) — transforming the global pattern of trade so substantially that China became the world's largest exporter of goods, fundamentally reshaping supply chains and competitive pressure across manufacturing industries worldwide.
Why the Pattern of Trade Matters for Individual Economies: a country's specific pattern of trade — which goods it exports, and to which markets — determines its vulnerability to particular shocks (e.g. a commodity-dependent exporter is exposed to volatile global commodity prices, Section 4) and shapes the domestic political debate over trade policy (Sections 6–7), since the costs and benefits of trade liberalisation or protection fall very differently depending on a country's specific trading pattern.
4.1.4

Terms of Trade

The terms of trade measure the rate at which a country's exports exchange for its imports — a concept with particular significance for commodity-dependent developing economies.

Terms of Trade
The ratio of a country's export prices to its import prices (often expressed as an index) — an improvement means a given quantity of exports now buys more imports than before; a deterioration means the opposite.

Worked Example — Calculating a Change in the Terms of Trade

A country's export price index rises from 100 to 108 over a year. Its import price index rises from 100 to 120 over the same period.

Terms of Trade Index = (Export Price Index ÷ Import Price Index) × 100 = (108 ÷ 120) × 100 = 90

Key point: the terms of trade have deteriorated from 100 to 90 — even though export prices rose in absolute terms, they rose by less than import prices, meaning the country must now export more to afford the same quantity of imports as before.

The Prebisch-Singer Condition: as examined in depth in the companion BIZ-OMICS Economic Development and Growth guide, the Prebisch-Singer hypothesis argues that countries specialising in primary commodity exports (raw materials, agricultural goods) tend to experience persistent long-run deterioration in their terms of trade relative to countries exporting manufactured goods — since global demand for raw commodities grows more slowly than demand for manufactured goods as incomes rise (low income elasticity of demand), while many developing countries simultaneously compete to export similar commodities, depressing prices further. This has been highly influential in shaping development strategy (Section 17), providing part of the historical justification for import-substitution industrialisation.
Cause of a Change in the Terms of TradeEffect
A rise in global demand for a country's exportsExport prices rise relative to import prices — terms of trade improve.
A rise in the price of imported raw materials/energyImport prices rise relative to export prices — terms of trade deteriorate.
Exchange rate appreciationCan improve the terms of trade by making imports cheaper in domestic currency, though this may also reduce export competitiveness (Section 9).
Productivity growth in export industriesCan allow export prices to fall while remaining profitable, which may worsen the terms of trade even as the country becomes genuinely more competitive.
Why an "Improving" Terms of Trade Isn't Always Good News: a rise in the terms of trade driven by strong global demand for a country's exports is generally beneficial, but a rise driven instead by a fall in the country's own import volumes (e.g. due to a domestic recession reducing import demand) reflects weakness, not strength — always examine the underlying cause of a terms of trade change before judging whether it is good or bad news for the economy.
4.1.5

Trading Blocs and the WTO

Countries can pursue trade liberalisation either multilaterally, through the World Trade Organisation, or regionally, by forming trading blocs with varying degrees of economic integration.

World Trade Organisation (WTO)
The principal international body overseeing global trade rules, negotiating multilateral trade liberalisation, and adjudicating trade disputes between member countries.
Type of Trading BlocKey FeatureExample
Preferential Trade AreaReduced (not eliminated) tariffs between members on some goods.Various regional agreements
Free Trade AreaNo tariffs between members, but each member sets its own external tariff on non-members.NAFTA/USMCA
Customs UnionNo tariffs between members, plus a common external tariff on non-members.The EU Customs Union
Common (Single) MarketA customs union plus free movement of capital and labour between members.The EU Single Market
Economic and Monetary UnionA common market plus a shared currency and unified monetary policy.The Eurozone (companion BIZ-OMICS Exchange Rates guide's currency union material)
Fig 1: The welfare effect of removing a tariff between trading partners. Eliminating the tariff lowers the price to domestic consumers, expands the quantity traded, and generates a net welfare gain — the foundation of the case for deeper economic integration through trading blocs.
Fig 2: Trade diversion. A country inside a trading bloc may switch its import source from a genuinely lower-cost external producer (e.g. Australia) to a higher-cost bloc member (e.g. the EU), simply because the bloc's common external tariff makes the external supplier artificially more expensive — a net welfare loss relative to genuinely free trade with the lowest-cost global producer, even though trade has been "created" within the bloc.
Trade Creation
Occurs when forming or joining a trading bloc shifts consumption from a less efficient domestic producer to a more efficient bloc-member producer — a genuine welfare gain.
Trade Diversion
Occurs when forming or joining a trading bloc shifts consumption from a more efficient non-member producer to a less efficient bloc-member producer, purely because of the bloc's external tariff — a net welfare loss relative to global free trade.
★ UK EXAMPLE: Brexit and Trade Diversion
As examined in depth in the companion BIZ-OMICS International Trade guide, leaving the EU Customs Union removed the UK from the EU's common external tariff, allowing it to independently negotiate trade deals and potentially reverse some pre-existing trade diversion by trading more freely with lower-cost non-EU producers — while simultaneously introducing new trade barriers (customs checks, rules of origin) with the EU itself, its largest trading partner, illustrating that leaving a trading bloc involves its own complex trade-offs, not simply a mirror image of joining one.
Exam Application: whether a specific trading bloc is, on balance, trade-creating or trade-diverting for a member country depends on the relative efficiency of its bloc partners versus the rest of the world — the strongest answers assess this empirically for the specific bloc and goods in question, rather than assuming all trading blocs are automatically beneficial simply because they increase trade between members.
4.1.6

Restrictions on Free Trade: Tariffs and Quotas

Protectionism — government measures to restrict imports and protect domestic industries — takes several distinct forms, each with different economic effects.

Protectionism
Government policy intended to restrict international trade, typically to protect domestic industries from foreign competition.
Tariff
A tax imposed on imported goods, raising their price to domestic consumers.
Quota
A physical limit on the quantity of a good that can be imported, regardless of price.
Fig 3: The effect of a tariff. The tariff raises the domestic price above the world price, reducing the quantity imported, increasing domestic production, reducing consumption, and generating tariff revenue for the government — but creating a net welfare loss overall.
Fig 4: The effect of an import quota. Like a tariff, a quota raises the domestic price and restricts imported quantity — but the resulting revenue (the gap between the world price and the higher domestic price on the restricted quantity) typically accrues to importers or foreign exporters holding quota licences, rather than to the domestic government as tariff revenue would.
Comparing Tariffs and QuotasTariffQuota
Government revenueYes — tariff revenue accrues to governmentNo direct government revenue (unless quota licences are auctioned)
Response to rising demandQuantity imported can still rise if demand increases, since the tariff is a price effect, not an absolute limitQuantity is capped absolutely, so rising demand simply raises domestic price further with no additional imports
TransparencyThe tax rate is generally public and transparentThe precise welfare cost can be less visible, particularly if licences are allocated opaquely
Argument For ProtectionArgument Against Protection
Infant industry protection — shields a new domestic industry until it achieves sufficient scale/experience to compete internationally.Protected industries can lack the competitive pressure to ever actually become efficient, remaining permanently reliant on protection ("sunset" rather than "infant" industries).
Protecting strategic industries — maintaining domestic capacity in sectors judged critical for national security (e.g. food, energy, defence).Retaliation risk — trading partners may impose their own tariffs in response, escalating into a damaging trade war (Section 7).
Preventing dumping — countering foreign firms selling below cost to eliminate domestic competition.Higher prices and reduced choice for domestic consumers, and reduced export competitiveness for domestic firms reliant on now-protected (and often costlier) imported inputs.
Protecting domestic employment — shielding jobs in import-competing industries.Contradicts the comparative advantage case for trade (Section 2), reducing overall global and domestic economic welfare in the long run.
4.1.6

Restrictions on Free Trade: Subsidies, Embargoes and Trade Wars

Beyond tariffs and quotas, governments can restrict or distort trade through domestic subsidies, outright trade bans, and the escalating retaliatory cycles of a trade war.

Trade Subsidy
A payment from government to domestic producers, lowering their costs and making them more competitive against imports, or more competitive as exporters, without directly taxing imports.
Trade Embargo
A complete government-imposed ban on trade with a specific country, typically imposed for political or security reasons rather than pure economic protection.
Fig 5: A production subsidy on a domestically-produced good. The subsidy lowers domestic producers' costs, increasing domestic output and reducing import volumes at the world price, without directly taxing consumers through a higher price — the cost instead falls on taxpayers funding the subsidy.
Fig 6: An export subsidy. Government payments to exporters lower their effective costs, allowing them to sell profitably at a lower price abroad, increasing export volumes — but at a direct cost to the domestic taxpayer, and a practice restricted or prohibited under WTO rules in many circumstances precisely because of the unfair competitive advantage it creates.
Why Trade Wars Rarely Have a Genuine Winner: a trade war — a cycle of escalating retaliatory tariffs between trading partners — typically leaves both economies worse off than before it began: domestic consumers in both countries face higher prices, and export industries in both countries lose access to markets they previously served competitively. Despite this, trade wars can still occur and persist, since the political benefits of being seen to "protect" a visible domestic industry can outweigh the more diffuse, less visible costs imposed on consumers and unrelated export industries — a classic case of concentrated benefits and dispersed costs shaping policy even where the aggregate economic case is weak.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: US-China Trade Tensions
Escalating tariffs between the United States and China from 2018 onward raised costs for businesses and consumers in both countries reliant on now-tariffed imports, while contributing to a broader restructuring of global supply chains as firms sought to reduce exposure to the tariffs — illustrating both the direct costs of a trade war and its longer-run effect of reshaping the global pattern of trade (Section 3) independently of underlying comparative advantage.
Exam Application: when evaluating any protectionist measure, always identify who specifically bears the cost (consumers via higher prices, taxpayers via subsidy funding, or trading partners via retaliation) and who specifically benefits (protected domestic producers, their employees) — the strongest answers avoid treating protectionism as either uniformly good or uniformly bad, instead weighing the specific, identifiable winners against the specific, identifiable losers in the case at hand.
4.1.7

The Balance of Payments

The balance of payments — a record of all financial transactions between a country's residents and the rest of the world — was introduced fully in Theme 2 as a measure of macroeconomic performance; here, it is examined specifically in its international economics context.

Cross-Reference to Theme 2: the full structure of the balance of payments — the current account (trade in goods, trade in services, primary income, secondary income), the capital/financial account, and the principle that the balance of payments must always balance overall — is covered in depth in Theme 2, Section 4, including the current account components diagram and the UK's persistent current account deficit case study. That material applies directly here.
International Economics ApplicationExplanation
Trade policy and the current accountProtectionist measures (Sections 6–7) directly affect the trade in goods and services components of the current account, by design.
Exchange rate adjustmentA floating exchange rate (Section 9) can adjust automatically to help correct a current account imbalance, a mechanism examined in full via the Marshall-Lerner condition and J-curve (Section 11).
Capital flows and developmentForeign direct investment inflows, recorded in the financial account, are a major channel through which developing economies access capital and technology (Section 17).
Why the Balance of Payments Sits in Both Themes: Theme 2 treats the balance of payments primarily as a UK macroeconomic performance indicator; Theme 4 treats it as part of the wider international economics framework — connecting trade policy (Sections 6–7), exchange rates (Sections 9–11), and international capital flows (Section 17) into a single coherent picture of how a country's international economic position is measured and managed.
4.1.8

Exchange Rates: Floating Systems

An exchange rate is the price of one currency in terms of another — under a floating exchange rate system, this price is determined purely by market demand and supply, with no central bank intervention.

Exchange Rate
The price of one currency expressed in terms of another currency.
Floating Exchange Rate
An exchange rate determined entirely by market forces of demand and supply for the currency, with no government or central bank intervention to influence its level.
Fig 7: Equilibrium in the market for a floating currency. The exchange rate is set purely by the intersection of demand for and supply of the currency, with no central bank intervention.
Determinant of Currency Demand/SupplyEffect on the Exchange Rate
Relative interest ratesHigher domestic interest rates attract foreign capital seeking better returns, increasing demand for the currency and causing appreciation.
Relative inflation ratesHigher domestic inflation than trading partners erodes competitiveness (Section 12), typically leading to depreciation over time.
Trade flowsA trade surplus increases foreign demand for the domestic currency (to pay for exports), tending to cause appreciation; a deficit tends to cause depreciation.
SpeculationExpectations of future currency movements can themselves drive current demand and supply, sometimes causing significant short-term volatility unrelated to underlying economic fundamentals.

Benefits of a Floating Exchange Rate

  • Automatic adjustment towards balance of payments equilibrium, without requiring active central bank intervention.
  • Frees monetary policy (companion Monetary Policy guide) to focus entirely on domestic objectives, since the exchange rate is not a policy target itself.
  • No need to hold large foreign currency reserves purely for exchange rate defence.

Drawbacks of a Floating Exchange Rate

  • Can be volatile, creating uncertainty for firms engaged in international trade and investment.
  • Vulnerable to speculative attack and sentiment-driven swings disconnected from economic fundamentals.
  • Offers no external anchor for controlling domestic inflation, unlike a credible fixed peg to a low-inflation currency.
Which Countries Float, and Why Development Matters: most major advanced economies (the US, UK, Eurozone, Japan) operate floating exchange rates, reflecting deep, liquid currency markets and strong policy credibility. Many developing and emerging economies instead adopt fixed or managed systems (Section 10), often because their currency markets are too thin and volatile for a clean float to function smoothly, or because a credible peg can help anchor inflation expectations in an economy with a history of high or volatile inflation.
4.1.8

Exchange Rates: Fixed Systems

Under a fixed exchange rate system, a government or central bank commits to maintaining the currency at a specific target rate, actively intervening in currency markets as needed to defend it.

Fixed Exchange Rate
An exchange rate pegged by government/central bank policy to a specific value (often against another currency, a basket of currencies, or gold), defended through active intervention.
Fig 8: Defending an overvalued fixed rate. At the fixed rate, quantity supplied of the currency exceeds quantity demanded — the central bank must absorb this excess supply by buying its own currency, spending foreign currency reserves in the process. This cannot continue indefinitely if reserves are limited.
Fig 9: A managed float (target zone). The rate can move freely with genuine market demand and supply anywhere inside a shaded band; the central bank intervenes only if the rate threatens to break through the ceiling or floor — a hybrid between a pure float and a rigidly fixed rate.
Fig 10: A managed float in practice over time. The rate wanders freely inside the permitted band; every time it touches the ceiling or floor, the central bank intervenes to push it back inside the range.

Benefits of a Fixed Exchange Rate

  • Provides certainty for firms trading and investing internationally, removing exchange rate risk from their planning.
  • Can act as a credible anchor against domestic inflation, particularly valuable for an economy with a history of high inflation.
  • Removes the possibility of destabilising currency speculation disconnected from fundamentals, provided the peg is credible.

Drawbacks of a Fixed Exchange Rate

  • Requires holding substantial foreign currency reserves to defend the rate, an ongoing opportunity cost.
  • Sacrifices independent monetary policy — interest rates must often be set to defend the currency peg rather than to address purely domestic objectives.
  • Vulnerable to a sudden, disorderly currency crisis if the peg becomes fundamentally unsustainable and market participants lose confidence in it (Section 11).
Devaluation
A deliberate reduction in the value of a fixed exchange rate by the government/central bank.
Revaluation
A deliberate increase in the value of a fixed exchange rate by the government/central bank.
Currency Union
The ultimate form of fixed exchange rate — member countries adopt a single shared currency entirely, eliminating exchange rate movements between them completely, but also eliminating independent national monetary policy (e.g. the Eurozone).
When Fixed Rates Break — Currency Crises: a fixed rate that becomes fundamentally misaligned with underlying economic conditions (an unsustainable peg) can collapse suddenly and disorderly once markets lose confidence that the central bank can or will continue defending it — historical episodes such as the UK's 1992 exit from the European Exchange Rate Mechanism ("Black Wednesday") illustrate how quickly a seemingly stable fixed rate can unravel once speculative pressure overwhelms available reserves.
4.1.8

Devaluation, Marshall-Lerner and the J-Curve

A devaluation is intended to improve a country's trade balance by making exports cheaper and imports dearer — but whether this actually happens, and how quickly, depends on a specific condition regarding demand elasticities.

The Marshall-Lerner Condition
A devaluation will improve the trade balance only if the sum of the price elasticities of demand for exports and imports exceeds 1 (in absolute value) — if demand for both is sufficiently inelastic, a devaluation can actually worsen the trade balance.

Worked Example — Applying the Marshall-Lerner Condition

Following a 10% devaluation, export demand rises by 4% (PED for exports = 0.4) and import demand falls by 3% (PED for imports = 0.3).

Sum of elasticities = 0.4 + 0.3 = 0.7

Key point: since 0.7 < 1, the Marshall-Lerner condition is not satisfied — demand for both exports and imports is too inelastic in this case, meaning the devaluation will actually worsen the trade balance in the short run, since the same (or only slightly changed) volume of imports now costs more in domestic currency, while export revenue does not rise enough to compensate.

Fig 11: The J-curve. The trade balance worsens immediately after devaluation (demand is inelastic in the short run — existing contracts and consumption habits take time to adjust), bottoms out, then crosses back through zero before moving into surplus in the long run, as demand for exports and imports becomes progressively more elastic once buyers and sellers have time to fully respond.
Why Short-Run Elasticities Differ From Long-Run Elasticities: immediately after a devaluation, existing supply contracts remain in place, consumers have not yet found new substitutes, and firms have not yet identified new suppliers or export markets — all of which take genuine time (renegotiating contracts, building new logistics and supply relationships, establishing trust with new trading partners). This is precisely why the Marshall-Lerner condition is far more likely to hold in the long run than the short run, producing the characteristic J-shaped time path of the trade balance rather than an immediate improvement.
Risk of DevaluationExplanation
Imported inflationA weaker currency immediately raises the domestic price of imports, potentially feeding into cost-push inflation (companion Inflation guide).
Competitive devaluationTrading partners may respond with their own devaluations to protect their competitiveness, leaving relative positions unchanged while raising global uncertainty.
Credibility damageAn unplanned or crisis-driven devaluation can severely damage market confidence in a government's commitment to future exchange rate and macroeconomic stability.
4.1.9

International Competitiveness

A country's international competitiveness — its ability to sell goods and services profitably in global markets — depends on both price and non-price factors, and directly shapes its trade performance and balance of payments position.

International Competitiveness
The ability of a country's firms to compete successfully in domestic and international markets against foreign rivals, reflecting both relative costs/prices and non-price factors such as quality and innovation.
Unit Labour Costs
The labour cost of producing one unit of output — rising unit labour costs (wages rising faster than productivity) directly erode price competitiveness.
Determinant of CompetitivenessExplanation
Relative unit labour costsIf domestic wages rise faster than productivity relative to competitor countries, unit labour costs rise, eroding price competitiveness.
The exchange rateA stronger currency makes exports more expensive in foreign currency terms, directly reducing price competitiveness (Sections 9–10).
Relative inflation ratesPersistently higher domestic inflation than trading partners steadily erodes competitiveness over time (Section 4's terms of trade).
Productivity growthFaster productivity growth allows a country to produce more competitively without needing to suppress wages.
Non-price factorsProduct quality, design, reliability, innovation, and branding all affect competitiveness independently of price — a key reason some high-wage economies remain highly competitive exporters despite high unit labour costs.
Infrastructure and institutionsReliable transport, energy, digital infrastructure, and the rule of law all reduce the effective cost of doing business, supporting competitiveness.
Why High-Wage Economies Can Remain Highly Competitive: Germany and Switzerland, among the world's highest-wage economies, have consistently ranked among the most competitive exporters globally — illustrating that price competitiveness (driven by unit labour costs and the exchange rate) is only part of the picture; strong non-price competitiveness (engineering quality, brand reputation, innovation, reliability) can more than offset a wage-cost disadvantage relative to lower-wage competitors.
Exam Application: when assessing a country's international competitiveness, always weigh price factors (unit labour costs, the exchange rate, relative inflation) against non-price factors (quality, innovation, reliability) explicitly — a common error is to treat competitiveness as determined by cost/price alone, when in practice many of the world's most successful exporters compete primarily on non-price factors.
4.2.1

Absolute and Relative Poverty

In a global context, the distinction between absolute and relative poverty takes on particular significance, since the scale of absolute poverty still faced by billions of people worldwide differs enormously from the relative poverty debates common in developed economies.

Absolute Poverty
A household is in absolute poverty if its income falls below a fixed, real-terms threshold representing the minimum needed to afford basic necessities (food, clean water, shelter) — globally, the World Bank's International Poverty Line is the standard benchmark, updated periodically for inflation and currently set at $2.15 per person per day (2017 PPP-adjusted prices).
Relative Poverty
A household is in relative poverty if its income falls below a fixed proportion (commonly 60%) of the contemporary median income within its own country — relative poverty can exist even in a wealthy country where no household faces genuine absolute deprivation.
TrendExplanation
Sharp fall in global absolute poverty since 1990The proportion of the world's population living in extreme (absolute) poverty has fallen dramatically since 1990, driven substantially by rapid growth in China, India, and other large emerging economies (companion BIZ-OMICS Economic Development and Growth guide).
Persistent concentration in Sub-Saharan AfricaDespite the overall global decline, extreme poverty remains heavily concentrated in Sub-Saharan Africa, which now accounts for a large and rising share of the world's remaining absolute poor, reflecting the region's specific development barriers (companion Economic Development and Growth guide).
Relative poverty persists even in high-income countriesDespite negligible absolute poverty by global standards, developed economies including the UK continue to report significant relative poverty rates (companion BIZ-OMICS Income, Wealth and Poverty guide covers this in UK-specific depth).
Why the Global Poverty Line Requires PPP Adjustment: the World Bank's International Poverty Line is expressed in purchasing-power-parity-adjusted dollars specifically because a nominal dollar figure would be meaningless across countries with vastly different price levels (companion BIZ-OMICS PPF, Circular Flow and National Income guide's PPP material) — $2.15 buys very different real quantities of food and basic necessities depending on local prices, so the PPP adjustment is essential to make the threshold represent a genuinely comparable standard of minimum subsistence across countries.
Cause of Global PovertyExplanation
Low productivity and weak economic growthEconomies with persistently low output growth struggle to raise average incomes sufficiently to lift large populations out of absolute poverty (companion Economic Development and Growth guide's growth barriers).
Conflict and political instabilityWar and instability destroy productive capacity, displace populations, and deter the investment needed for growth.
Weak institutions and governancePoor property rights, corruption, and weak rule of law depress investment and misdirect resources away from productive uses.
Limited access to education and healthcareConstrains human capital development, perpetuating low productivity across generations.
Adverse terms of tradeCommodity-dependent economies facing the Prebisch-Singer deterioration (Section 4) see their real incomes squeezed over time even without any domestic policy failure.
4.2.2

Inequality

Alongside poverty, inequality — how income and wealth are distributed, independent of the absolute standard of living — is a distinct and increasingly prominent concern in the global economy.

Fig 12: The Lorenz curve. The 45-degree diagonal represents perfect equality; the bowed curve shows the actual cumulative distribution of income across a population — the further the curve bows away from the diagonal, the more unequal the distribution. This tool applies equally to measuring inequality within a single country or comparing inequality between countries globally.
Fig 13: The Gini coefficient. Gini = Area A ÷ (Area A + Area B) — a value of 0 represents perfect equality, and 1 represents perfect inequality (one individual holding all income). The Gini coefficient allows direct numerical comparison of inequality levels between different countries.
Dimension of Global InequalityExplanation
Inequality within countriesMeasured using the same tools (Lorenz curve, Gini coefficient) applied to a single country's income or wealth distribution — covered in full UK-specific depth in the companion BIZ-OMICS Income, Wealth and Poverty guide.
Inequality between countriesThe gap in average income (GDP/GNI per capita, Theme 2, Section 1) between the world's richest and poorest countries — historically very large, though narrowing somewhat as emerging economies have grown faster than advanced economies in recent decades.
Global inequality (combining both)Measures inequality across the entire world population as if it were a single country, combining both within-country and between-country inequality — a composite view increasingly used to assess whether globalisation (Section 1) has narrowed or widened the gap between the world's richest and poorest individuals overall.
Why Between-Country Inequality Has Narrowed While Within-Country Inequality Has Often Widened: rapid growth in large emerging economies, particularly China and India, has significantly narrowed the gap in average income between countries over recent decades (companion Economic Development and Growth guide) — but within many of these same countries, and within many advanced economies simultaneously, income and wealth inequality has widened, as the gains from growth and globalisation have not been evenly distributed across the population (Section 1's globalisation costs). This makes the overall global inequality picture considerably more nuanced than either trend viewed in isolation.
Cause of Rising Global InequalityExplanation
Skill-biased technological changeAutomation and digital technology have increased demand for highly skilled workers while reducing demand for routine labour, widening wage gaps (companion BIZ-OMICS Labour Market guide).
Globalisation's uneven effectsTrade and capital mobility (Section 1) have benefited owners of capital and highly skilled workers more than workers in import-competing industries.
Differential access to educationUnequal access to quality education perpetuates income differences across generations, both within and between countries.
Tax competition and MNC tax avoidanceMobile capital and profit-shifting by multinationals (Section 1) can erode the tax base needed to fund redistribution (Section 22), particularly in developing economies with weaker tax enforcement capacity.
Exam Application: when discussing global inequality, always specify which dimension is being addressed — within-country, between-country, or combined global inequality — since policy addressing one dimension (e.g. progressive taxation for within-country inequality) may have little bearing on another (e.g. between-country inequality requires convergence in growth rates, not domestic redistribution).
4.3.1

Measures of Development

Economic development is a broader concept than economic growth — measuring genuine improvement in human welfare and living standards, not simply a rise in output.

Economic Growth
An increase in a country's real output (real GDP) over time.
Economic Development
A broader improvement in human welfare, encompassing rising living standards, better health and education outcomes, reduced poverty, and greater economic opportunity — growth can occur without development if its benefits are not translated into genuine welfare improvements.
ClassificationDefinition
LEDC (Less Economically Developed Country)A country with low income per capita and typically weaker health, education, and infrastructure indicators.
MEDC (More Economically Developed Country)A high-income country with strong health, education, and infrastructure indicators.
NIC (Newly Industrialised Country)A country that has undergone rapid industrialisation and growth, transitioning from LEDC towards MEDC status (e.g. South Korea, historically; more recently applied to countries such as Vietnam).
MeasureWhat It CapturesLimitation
GDP/GNI per capitaAverage income per person (Theme 2, Section 1).Says nothing about distribution, health, or education directly.
Human Development Index (HDI)Combines GNI per capita with life expectancy and education (literacy/school enrolment) into a single composite index.Still excludes inequality, environmental sustainability, and political freedom.
Genuine Progress Indicator (GPI)Adjusts GDP for income inequality, environmental damage, and unpaid household labour.Less standardised and harder to compare consistently across countries than GDP or HDI.
Why Growth and Development Can Diverge — The Middle-Income Trap: some countries achieve substantial growth in average income without corresponding improvements in institutional quality, income distribution, or economic diversification — becoming stuck in a "middle-income trap," unable to progress further towards high-income status because the low-cost, low-skill strategies that drove their initial growth no longer provide a competitive advantage once wages rise, while they have not yet developed the innovation capacity and institutional strength needed to compete as an advanced, high-income economy.
4.3.2

Factors Influencing Growth and Development

Some developing economies have achieved rapid, sustained growth and development, while others have remained persistently poor — reflecting a combination of structural, institutional, and external barriers.

Barrier to DevelopmentExplanation
Weak institutions and governanceCorruption, weak property rights, and unreliable rule of law deter investment and misdirect resources — widely regarded as one of the single most important determinants of long-run development outcomes.
Primary product dependencyHeavy reliance on commodity exports exposes an economy to volatile prices and the long-run terms of trade deterioration described by the Prebisch-Singer condition (Section 4).
Low savings and investment (the "vicious cycle of poverty")Low incomes leave little surplus for savings, constraining the investment needed to raise future productivity and incomes — a self-reinforcing trap without external capital or intervention.
Poor infrastructureInadequate transport, energy, and communications infrastructure raises the cost of doing business and deters both domestic and foreign investment.
Limited human capitalWeak education and healthcare systems constrain workforce productivity and capacity for innovation.
Conflict and political instabilityWar, civil conflict, and political instability directly destroy productive capacity and deter investment.
Debt burdensHigh external debt service costs can crowd out spending on health, education, and infrastructure (Section 17).
Dutch Disease — a specific resource-driven barrier: a country discovers a valuable natural resource (e.g. oil or gas) → resource exports surge, generating a large inflow of foreign currency → the exchange rate appreciates sharply as demand for the domestic currency rises to pay for these exports (Section 9) → the stronger currency makes the country's other exports (manufacturing, agriculture) less competitive internationally, while making imports cheaper → non-resource export sectors decline, unable to compete → the economy becomes increasingly dependent on the resource sector alone → when the resource boom eventually ends, the economy is left without a diversified productive base to fall back on.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Norway's Sovereign Wealth Fund as a Dutch Disease Defence
Norway largely avoided the worst effects of Dutch Disease following its North Sea oil discoveries by saving a large share of oil revenue in a sovereign wealth fund rather than spending it all domestically — limiting the currency appreciation and inflationary pressure that would otherwise have damaged its non-oil economy, and providing a widely cited model for other resource-rich economies seeking to avoid the same trap.
Driving Force for DevelopmentExplanation
Strong institutions and governanceSecure property rights, low corruption, and reliable rule of law encourage investment and long-run planning.
Investment in human capitalStrong education and healthcare systems raise workforce productivity and support innovation.
Export diversificationReducing dependency on primary commodities lowers vulnerability to terms of trade deterioration and volatile commodity prices.
Infrastructure investmentReliable transport, energy, and digital infrastructure lower business costs and attract investment.
Political and macroeconomic stabilityPredictable policy and stable institutions give firms the confidence to make long-term investment commitments.
4.3.3

Strategies Influencing Growth and Development

Economists and policymakers have proposed several distinct models and strategies for promoting growth and development, each with genuine insights but also significant limitations.

The Harrod-Domar Model
A model proposing that a country's growth rate depends on its savings ratio divided by its capital-output ratio (Growth Rate = Savings Ratio ÷ Capital-Output Ratio) — implying that raising domestic savings and investment is the key lever for accelerating growth.
Limitations of the Harrod-Domar Model: the model assumes a fixed capital-output ratio and largely ignores the role of technology, human capital, and institutional quality in determining growth — meaning it can substantially overstate the growth achievable from savings and investment alone, without complementary improvements in these other areas.
Rostow's StageCharacteristics
1. Traditional societySubsistence agriculture, limited technology, low productivity.
2. Preconditions for take-offEmerging commercial agriculture, early infrastructure investment, growing trade.
3. Take-offRapid industrialisation in leading sectors, rising investment rates.
4. Drive to maturityDiversification across industries, widespread adoption of modern technology.
5. Age of high mass consumptionDurable goods and services dominate the economy; high living standards.
Limitations of Rostow's Model: Rostow's model has been widely criticised for assuming all countries can and should follow the same linear development path historically taken by Western economies, ignoring the very different global trade conditions, institutional starting points, and geopolitical context facing developing countries today compared with 19th-century Europe or America.
StrategyMechanismLimitation/Risk
Foreign Direct Investment (FDI)Attracts capital, technology, and management expertise from abroad, potentially accelerating growth beyond what domestic savings alone could fund.Profits may be repatriated rather than reinvested locally; can create enclave economies with limited spillover to the rest of the domestic economy.
External debt and aidProvides capital to fund infrastructure and development spending beyond domestic tax revenue and savings.Debt service costs can become an unsustainable burden (Section 16); aid can create dependency or be misdirected by weak governance.
Import-substitution industrialisationProtects domestic industries (Sections 6–7) to allow them to develop before facing full international competition, motivated partly by the Prebisch-Singer concern (Section 4).Protected industries can remain permanently uncompetitive without the discipline of international competition.
Export-oriented industrialisationActively promotes manufactured exports, exposing domestic industries to competitive discipline from the start (the strategy widely credited with East Asian NICs' rapid development).Requires sufficient initial competitiveness and infrastructure to succeed in export markets.
★ CASE STUDY: South Korea's Development Strategy
South Korea's rapid post-war development is widely attributed to a deliberately interventionist strategy — combining subsidised credit, targeted support for specific export industries, and state-backed conglomerates (chaebols) — rather than a purely free-market approach, illustrating that successful development strategies have varied substantially rather than following a single universal formula, directly contrasting with Rostow's implied single linear path.
Exam Application: when evaluating a development strategy, always weigh it against the specific barriers (Section 16) it is intended to address in a named country's context, and note whether historical models (Harrod-Domar, Rostow) fully account for modern complications such as institutional quality, human capital, and global competitive conditions that these models often simplify away or ignore entirely.
4.4.1

Role of Financial Markets

Financial markets channel funds from savers to borrowers, playing an essential role in enabling investment, growth, and the efficient allocation of capital throughout the economy.

Financial Markets
Markets in which financial assets (money, bonds, shares, foreign currency) are bought and sold, connecting those with surplus funds (savers) to those needing funds (borrowers and investors).
Money Market
A market for short-term borrowing and lending (typically under one year), such as short-term government and corporate debt.
Capital Market
A market for long-term finance, including the stock market (equity) and the bond market (long-term debt).
Foreign Exchange (FOREX) Market
The market in which currencies are bought and sold, determining exchange rates (Sections 9–11).
Function of Financial MarketsExplanation
Facilitating savingProvide a return on household and firm savings, encouraging the deferral of consumption.
Lending to businesses and individualsChannel savings towards firms seeking investment finance and households seeking mortgages or consumer credit.
Facilitating exchangeEnable payments and the exchange of goods, services, and assets, both domestically and internationally.
Providing forward marketsAllow firms to hedge against future price and exchange rate risk.
Providing a market for equitiesAllow firms to raise long-term capital by selling ownership stakes, and allow savers to share in business profits.
Debt Finance
Borrowing that must be repaid with interest (e.g. loans, bonds) — the lender has no ownership stake in the business.
Equity Finance
Raising finance by selling ownership shares in a business — shareholders share in profits (dividends) and business risk, but the firm has no fixed repayment obligation.
Primary Market
Where new financial securities (shares, bonds) are issued and sold for the first time, with proceeds going directly to the issuing firm or government.
Secondary Market
Where existing financial securities are subsequently bought and sold between investors — proceeds go to the selling investor, not the original issuer, but secondary market liquidity is essential for making primary market issuance attractive in the first place.

Worked Example — Bond Yields and Prices

A bond has a face value of £1,000 and pays a fixed annual coupon of £50 (a 5% coupon rate). Its market price falls to £800.

Current yield = Annual coupon ÷ Market price = £50 ÷ £800 = 6.25%

Key point: as the bond's market price falls, its yield rises, since the fixed £50 coupon now represents a larger percentage return relative to the lower price paid — this inverse relationship between bond prices and yields (effectively, interest rates) is fundamental to understanding how monetary policy (companion Monetary Policy guide) transmits through financial markets.

Why Financial Markets Matter for the Whole Economy: well-functioning financial markets allow savings to be efficiently channelled towards their most productive investment uses, supporting economic growth (Theme 2, Section 16) — but as Section 19 examines, financial markets are also prone to distinctive forms of market failure that can, in extreme cases, threaten the stability of the entire economy.
4.4.2

Market Failure in the Financial Sector

Financial markets are prone to distinctive forms of market failure, rooted in information problems and the unique fragility of bank business models — with potentially severe consequences for the whole economy.

Maturity Transformation
The core function (and vulnerability) of commercial banking: banks borrow short-term (via instantly-withdrawable deposits) and lend long-term (via mortgages and business loans) — profitable in normal times, but creating a structural vulnerability if many depositors demand their money back simultaneously.
Bank Run
A situation where a large number of depositors, fearing a bank's insolvency, simultaneously attempt to withdraw their funds — since banks hold only a fraction of deposits as readily available liquid reserves (having lent most of the rest out long-term), a sufficiently large bank run can force even a fundamentally solvent bank into failure.
Moral Hazard
A situation where a party takes on excessive risk because they do not bear the full consequences of that risk — banks considered "too big to fail," expecting government rescue if they get into difficulty, may take on greater risks than they otherwise would.
Systemic Risk
The risk that the failure of one financial institution triggers a cascading failure across the wider financial system, due to interconnections (interbank lending, shared exposures) between institutions.
Cause of Financial Market FailureExplanation
Asymmetric informationBorrowers typically know more about their true creditworthiness and intentions than lenders, creating adverse selection (riskier borrowers disproportionately seek loans) and moral hazard (borrowers may take on excessive risk once funds are secured) problems.
Negative externalities of bank failureA bank failure imposes costs far beyond the bank's own shareholders — depositors, other financial institutions, and the wider economy (via reduced lending and confidence) all bear costs the failing bank does not fully internalise.
Speculation and herding behaviourFinancial markets can be prone to asset price bubbles, as investors follow prevailing trends rather than fundamental value, followed by sharp, destabilising corrections.
"Too big to fail" moral hazardLarge banks may take excessive risks, anticipating that government cannot allow them to fail without unacceptable damage to the wider economy.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Silicon Valley Bank (2023)
The rapid collapse of Silicon Valley Bank illustrated maturity transformation risk directly — the bank had invested a large share of its deposits in long-term government bonds, whose market value fell sharply as interest rates rose; once depositors (heavily concentrated among technology start-ups with large, easily-moved balances) began withdrawing funds rapidly amid concerns about the bank's solvency, it was forced to sell these bonds at a loss to meet withdrawals, accelerating rather than resolving the crisis — a modern illustration of a classic bank run dynamic, transmitted at digital speed through online banking and social media.
Why Financial Market Failure Justifies Special Regulatory Treatment: because a single bank failure can generate severe negative externalities for the wider economy through systemic risk, financial markets are subject to far more extensive regulation than most other industries (Section 20) — reflecting the judgement that the potential cost of financial market failure to the wider economy vastly exceeds the cost of failure in most other sectors.
4.4.3

Role of Central Banks

Beyond setting monetary policy (companion BIZ-OMICS Monetary Policy guide), central banks play a central role in regulating and safeguarding the stability of the financial system as a whole.

UK Regulatory BodyRole
The Bank of EnglandSets monetary policy via the MPC, and holds overall responsibility for UK financial stability.
Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA)Regulates individual banks, insurers, and major investment firms to ensure they are safely and prudently run, with adequate capital and liquidity.
Financial Policy Committee (FPC)Monitors and addresses risks to the financial system as a whole (systemic risk), rather than individual firms.
Financial Conduct Authority (FCA)Regulates the conduct of financial firms towards consumers and markets, focusing on fair treatment and market integrity rather than firm-level financial safety.
Capital Requirements
Rules requiring banks to hold a minimum proportion of their assets as loss-absorbing capital (shareholder funds), providing a buffer against losses before depositors or taxpayers are at risk.
Liquidity Requirements
Rules requiring banks to hold a minimum proportion of readily available liquid assets, reducing vulnerability to a bank run (Section 19).
Post-Crisis Regulatory Reform: following the 2008 financial crisis, UK and international regulators significantly raised capital and liquidity requirements for banks, introduced more rigorous stress testing of major institutions, and separated (ring-fenced) retail banking from riskier investment banking activities within the same banking group — reforms intended directly to address the "too big to fail" moral hazard problem and reduce the systemic risk exposed so severely in 2008.
Central Bank Tool for Financial StabilityPurpose
Lender of last resortThe central bank provides emergency liquidity to a fundamentally solvent bank facing a temporary liquidity crisis (a bank run), preventing an otherwise-avoidable failure.
Macroprudential regulationTools such as counter-cyclical capital buffers, requiring banks to hold more capital during a credit boom, when systemic risk is building, and allowing this buffer to be released during a downturn.
Stress testingRegularly testing major banks against severe hypothetical economic scenarios, ensuring they hold sufficient capital to survive a genuine crisis.
The Cost of Intervention — Bailouts: the 2008 rescue of UK banks including RBS and Lloyds Banking Group (companion BIZ-OMICS Income, Wealth and Poverty guide's public ownership material) involved substantial direct taxpayer cost and a significant expansion of government debt (Section 23) — but the alternative, allowing systemically important banks to fail outright, was judged likely to impose even greater economic costs through the systemic risk channel (Section 19), illustrating the difficult trade-off central banks and governments face between the moral hazard cost of bailouts and the systemic cost of allowing failure.
4.5.1

Public Expenditure

Government spending — a core component of aggregate demand (Theme 2, Section 8) — serves multiple distinct purposes beyond short-run demand management, from correcting market failure to redistributing income.

Rationale for Public ExpenditureExplanation
Providing public goodsGoods the free market would under-provide or fail to provide at all (companion BIZ-OMICS Market Failure guide) — national defence, street lighting, flood defences.
Correcting externalities and information failureFunding merit goods such as education and healthcare, which the market would otherwise under-provide.
Redistributing incomeWelfare payments and benefits directly redistribute income towards lower-income households (companion BIZ-OMICS Income, Wealth and Poverty guide).
Managing aggregate demandDiscretionary spending changes can be used to stimulate a weak economy (Theme 2, Section 21) or support automatic stabilisers during a downturn.
Supporting long-run growthInfrastructure, education, and R&D spending can raise long-run productive capacity (interventionist supply-side policy, Theme 2, Section 22).
Type of SpendingExamples
Current spendingRecurring spending on day-to-day operation of public services — public sector wages, medicines, consumables.
Capital spendingSpending on long-lived assets — new hospitals, roads, schools, and infrastructure.
Transfer paymentsPayments with no corresponding output (pensions, unemployment benefits) — redistributive rather than a direct purchase of goods/services, and therefore excluded from G in the AD identity (Theme 2, Section 8).
Cross-Reference — Crowding Out: as examined in Theme 2, Section 8, government spending funded by borrowing can raise interest rates in the loanable funds market, potentially crowding out private investment — a key evaluative consideration whenever assessing the wisdom of expanding public expenditure, alongside the opportunity cost of the specific taxation (Section 22) or borrowing (Section 23) used to fund it.
Global Context — Spending Priorities Vary by Development Level: the composition of public expenditure varies substantially with a country's stage of development (Section 15) — many developing economies prioritise basic infrastructure, primary education, and public health spending with high social returns, while advanced economies typically allocate a larger share to pensions, healthcare for ageing populations, and debt interest, reflecting their different demographic and developmental priorities.
4.5.2

Taxation

Taxation funds public expenditure (Section 21) while also serving as a policy tool in its own right — its structure and rates have significant effects on incentives, equity, and the international competitiveness of an economy.

Direct Tax
A tax levied directly on income or wealth (e.g. income tax, corporation tax), paid directly by the individual or firm on whom it is levied.
Indirect Tax
A tax levied on spending (e.g. VAT, excise duties), collected by the seller but ultimately borne partly or wholly by the consumer through a higher price (companion BIZ-OMICS Market Failure guide's tax incidence analysis).
Fig 14: The three tax structures compared, by average rate of tax paid as income rises. A progressive tax takes a rising share of income as income rises; a regressive tax takes a falling share; a proportional tax takes the same share regardless of income level.
Tax StructureDefinitionUK Example
ProgressiveAverage tax rate rises as income rises.UK income tax, with rising marginal rate bands.
RegressiveAverage tax rate falls as income rises.VAT and other flat-rate indirect taxes, since lower-income households spend a larger share of their income on taxed goods.
ProportionalAverage tax rate stays constant regardless of income.A flat-rate tax with no exemptions or bands.
Fig 15: The Laffer Curve. At a 0% tax rate, revenue is zero; at a 100% rate, revenue also collapses to zero, since nobody has any incentive to earn taxable income at all — between these extremes, revenue first rises then eventually falls as tax rates increase, since sufficiently high rates increasingly discourage work, investment, and honest reporting of income, or drive economic activity abroad or into the informal economy.
Why the Laffer Curve Matters for Global Tax Competition: the Laffer Curve's logic underpins much of the international tax competition observed among governments seeking to attract mobile capital and multinational investment (Section 1) — cutting corporation tax rates can, if a country is on the "wrong side" of its own Laffer curve, actually raise revenue by attracting more taxable economic activity than the lower rate would suggest, though the empirical evidence on exactly where any country sits on this curve remains genuinely contested.
Consideration in Tax Policy DesignExplanation
Equity vs efficiency trade-offMore progressive taxation improves equity (companion Income, Wealth and Poverty guide) but can reduce incentives to work, invest, or declare income at the margin.
International tax competitionMobile capital and highly skilled workers can relocate to lower-tax jurisdictions, constraining how progressive or high a country's taxes can practically be (Section 1's globalisation costs).
MNC profit shiftingMultinational tax avoidance via transfer pricing (Section 1) erodes the domestic tax base, a key driver of recent international efforts towards a global minimum corporate tax rate.
4.5.3

Public Sector Finances

The gap between government spending (Section 21) and tax revenue (Section 22) determines the government's budget balance, and its accumulation over time forms the national debt — with important implications for long-run fiscal sustainability.

Budget Deficit
A situation where government spending exceeds tax revenue in a given year, requiring the government to borrow to cover the shortfall.
Budget Surplus
A situation where tax revenue exceeds government spending in a given year.
National Debt
The total accumulated stock of government borrowing built up over time from successive annual deficits (partially offset by any surpluses).
Type of DeficitDefinition
Cyclical deficitThe portion of the deficit caused by the current position in the economic cycle (Theme 2, Section 18) — a negative output gap automatically raises benefit spending and lowers tax revenue, widening the deficit temporarily.
Structural deficitThe portion of the deficit that would persist even at full employment/potential output — reflecting a genuine, ongoing mismatch between spending commitments and tax revenue, not merely the current point in the economic cycle.
Why the Cyclical/Structural Distinction Matters: a cyclical deficit is generally not a cause for policy concern, since it should narrow automatically as the economy recovers (Theme 2, Section 21's automatic stabilisers) — but a structural deficit indicates a more fundamental imbalance requiring deliberate policy action (spending cuts or tax rises) to address, since it will not resolve itself through growth alone.
ConsiderationExplanation
Debt-to-GDP ratioNational debt is typically expressed relative to GDP, since a country's ability to service debt depends on the size of its economy, not the absolute debt figure alone.
Bond market confidenceGovernment borrowing is financed by selling bonds (Section 18); if investors lose confidence in a government's ability to repay, they demand higher yields, raising the cost of servicing the debt further — potentially triggering a self-reinforcing debt crisis.
Fiscal rulesSelf-imposed targets (e.g. limiting the deficit or debt-to-GDP ratio) intended to maintain market confidence and fiscal discipline, though credibility depends on a government's willingness to actually follow them even when politically difficult.
Intergenerational equityBorrowing today shifts some of the burden of current spending onto future taxpayers, who will service and eventually repay the debt — a key normative consideration in debates over the appropriate scale of government borrowing.
Cross-Reference: the Keynesian case for temporary deficits during a downturn, the crowding-out/crowding-in debate, Ricardian equivalence, and the real-world evidence on fiscal multipliers during austerity are all examined in full depth in the companion BIZ-OMICS Fiscal Policy guide and in Theme 2, Sections 15 and 21.
4.5.4

Macroeconomic Policy in a Global Context

In an increasingly globalised economy (Section 1), domestic macroeconomic policy cannot be designed in isolation — international capital mobility, trade linkages, and policy spillovers from other countries all constrain and shape a government's policy choices.

Global Constraint on Domestic PolicyExplanation
Capital mobility and tax competitionMobile capital and MNCs can relocate to lower-tax jurisdictions (Section 22), constraining how high a government can practically set corporation tax without losing investment and revenue.
Exchange rate and monetary policy interactionUnder a floating exchange rate (Section 9), domestic interest rate changes affect the exchange rate, which in turn affects net trade (Theme 2, Section 9) — meaning monetary policy transmission is never purely domestic in an open economy.
International policy coordination and spilloversMajor economies' monetary and fiscal policy choices affect global capital flows, commodity prices, and exchange rates, creating spillover effects that other countries must factor into their own policy decisions.
Global financial contagionFinancial market failure (Section 19) in one major economy can transmit rapidly to others through interconnected financial markets, as seen in the 2008 global financial crisis.
Trade policy interdependenceUnilateral protectionist measures (Sections 6–7) can provoke retaliation, meaning trade policy choices must account for other countries' likely responses, not just their direct domestic effect.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: The Global Minimum Corporate Tax
Over 140 countries agreed in 2021 to implement a global minimum corporate tax rate of 15%, directly intended to limit the international tax competition (Section 22) and MNC profit-shifting (Section 1) that had previously allowed multinationals to systematically minimise their tax liability by shifting profits to low-tax jurisdictions — illustrating how genuinely global coordination can be required to address a problem that no single country could solve acting alone, since unilateral action by any one country would simply divert investment towards remaining low-tax competitors.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: 2008 Financial Crisis Contagion
The 2008 financial crisis, originating in the US subprime mortgage market, transmitted rapidly to banks and economies worldwide through interconnected global financial markets (Section 19) — UK banks with exposure to related assets required substantial government bailouts, and the resulting global recession depressed demand for UK exports, illustrating how a shock originating in a single country's financial sector can rapidly become a genuinely global macroeconomic event.
Exam Application: when analysing UK macroeconomic policy, always consider whether and how the specific global context — capital mobility, exchange rate effects, international policy coordination, or global financial/trade linkages — constrains or shapes the policy's likely effectiveness, rather than analysing domestic policy purely in isolation, as this Theme's title deliberately signals is the required perspective throughout.
Exam Prep

Exam Technique & Model Chains of Reasoning

The Golden Chain: From Comparative Advantage to Trade Diversion

Two countries have different opportunity costs of production, giving each comparative advantage in a different good (Section 2) → specialising according to comparative advantage and trading raises total combined output for both countries → but if one country joins a trading bloc with a common external tariff (Section 5), it may be forced to import from a higher-cost bloc partner rather than the genuinely lowest-cost global producer → this is trade diversion, a net welfare loss relative to free trade with the world's most efficient producer, even though trade between bloc members has technically "increased" → whether joining the bloc is, on balance, beneficial depends on whether trade creation (switching from inefficient domestic production to efficient bloc-partner production) outweighs this trade diversion cost.

The Golden Chain: From a Devaluation to Its Eventual Effect on the Trade Balance

A country's currency devalues (Sections 9–10) → in the short run, demand for exports and imports is highly inelastic, since existing contracts and consumption habits have not yet adjusted → the trade balance initially worsens, since the same import volumes now cost more in domestic currency while export revenue barely rises (the Marshall-Lerner condition is not met in the short run) → over time, firms identify new suppliers and customers, renegotiate contracts, and build new supply chains → demand for exports and imports becomes progressively more elastic → the Marshall-Lerner condition is eventually satisfied → the trade balance improves and may move into surplus → this whole process traces out the characteristic J-curve (Fig. 11).

The Golden Chain: From MNC Tax Avoidance to Global Policy Coordination

Globalisation and capital mobility (Section 1) allow multinational corporations to shift reported profits to low-tax jurisdictions via transfer pricing → this erodes the tax base of higher-tax countries where genuine economic activity actually occurs → any single country attempting to unilaterally raise corporation tax risks simply driving investment towards lower-tax competitors (the Laffer Curve logic of Section 22, applied internationally) → this creates a collective action problem no single country can solve alone → international coordination (Section 24) — such as the 2021 global minimum corporate tax agreement — becomes necessary to prevent a continued "race to the bottom" in corporate tax rates.

Key Exam Tips

1

Always distinguish absolute advantage from comparative advantage explicitly (Section 2) — a country can gain from trade even without absolute advantage in anything, and this distinction is frequently and specifically tested.

2

For any Marshall-Lerner/J-curve question (Section 11), always address both the short-run elasticity problem and the long-run adjustment explicitly — a common error is to assume a devaluation improves the trade balance immediately.

3

Distinguish absolute poverty (a fixed real threshold, globally PPP-adjusted) from relative poverty (tied to a country's own median income) explicitly (Section 13) — and remember these can move in different directions simultaneously.

4

When discussing global inequality (Section 14), always specify whether you mean within-country, between-country, or combined global inequality, since these can move in different directions at the same time.

5

For development strategy questions (Section 17), name the specific historical model (Harrod-Domar, Rostow) being applied or critiqued, and note its specific limitations rather than treating development strategy as a single undifferentiated topic.

6

For questions on the state's role in the macroeconomy (Section 4.5), always distinguish cyclical from structural deficits (Section 23) before evaluating whether current borrowing is a policy concern — this distinction is one of the most consistently rewarded pieces of analysis in this sub-topic.

7

Theme 4 explicitly requires a global perspective — for any policy discussion, look for an opportunity to reference international capital mobility, exchange rate effects, or global policy coordination (Section 24) explicitly, since this is precisely what distinguishes Theme 4 analysis from the domestically-focused analysis expected in Theme 2.

BIZ-OMICS | biz-omics.co.uk | Edexcel A-Level Economics A | Theme 4: A Global Perspective
